subject: Careers
topic: Bias Mini-Unit — Day 1: Bias Basics and Common Traps

worksheet.title: Bias Mini-Unit — Day 1: Bias Basics and Common Traps
anchor: Learning goal: I can explain what cognitive bias is and identify confirmation bias, availability bias, and overconfidence bias.
Use this worksheet during the mini-lesson, then move into work time on your career presentation.

questions.q_text: Quick start: If you already like a career, what kind of information might you notice first? Why?
Key notes: Confirmation bias means looking for or trusting information that matches what you already believe. Write your own example or note.
Key notes: Availability bias means judging something by the example that comes to mind fastest, even if it is unusual. Write your own example or note.
Key notes: Overconfidence bias means feeling more certain than the evidence actually supports. Write your own example or note.

Practice: A student only writes down the positives of a career they already like. Bias: ____________________
One dramatic story about a workplace makes a job seem typical all the time. Bias: ____________________
A presenter says their career is clearly the best without checking enough sources. Bias: ____________________
Apply it to your career presentation: What is one way confirmation bias could show up in your research?
Apply it to your career presentation: What is one fact you should check so you do not rely on one dramatic example?
Apply it to your career presentation: Write one sentence you could use instead of 'This is obviously the best job.'
Exit reflection: Which of today's biases are you most likely to make in your own presentation? What will you do to reduce it?

self_check: I can name confirmation bias, availability bias, and overconfidence bias.
I connected at least one bias to my own career presentation work.
I am ready to continue revising or building my presentation.
